elements.sent-2411-001: Parts of this work have appeared in the proceedings of IEEE International Symposium on Information Theory (ISIT) 2024 as well as International Zurich Seminar (IZS) 2024.
elements.sent-2411-002: A fundamental problem in decentralized networked systems (such as for sensing or control) is to coordinate actions of different agents so that they reach a state of agreement.
elements.sent-2411-003: In such applications, it is additionally desirable that the actions at various nodes may not be anticipated by malicious eavesdroppers.
elements.sent-2411-004: Motivated by this, we investigate the problem of secure multi-terminal strong coordination aided by a multiple-access wiretap channel (MAC-WT).
elements.sent-2411-005: In this setup, independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.) copies of correlated sources are observed by two transmitters who encode the channel inputs to the MAC-WT.
elements.sent-2411-006: The legitimate receiver observing the channel output and side information correlated with the sources must produce approximately i.i.d.
elements.sent-2411-007: Furthermore, we demand that an external eavesdropper learns essentially nothing about the sources and the simulated output sequence by observing its own MAC-WT output.
elements.sent-2411-008: This setting is aided by the presence of independent pairwise shared randomness between each encoder and the legitimate decoder, that is unavailable to the eavesdropper.
elements.sent-2411-009: We derive an achievable rate region based on a combination of coordination coding and wiretap coding, along with an outer bound.
elements.sent-2411-010: The inner bound is shown to be tight and a complete characterization is derived for the special case when the sources are conditionally independent given the decoder side information and the legitimate channel is composed of deterministic links.
elements.sent-2411-011: Further, we also analyze a more general scenario with possible encoder cooperation, where one of the encoders can non-causally crib from the other encoder's input, for which an achievable rate region is proposed.
elements.sent-2411-012: We then explicitly compute the rate regions for an example both with and without cribbing between the encoders, and demonstrate that cribbing strictly improves upon the achievable rate region.
elements.sent-2411-013: Distributed simulation of correlated randomness across different nodes in a network is useful in several settings, like cryptographic protocols and distributed computation tasks~\cite{ahlswede1993common,ahlswede1998common .}
elements.sent-2411-014: The role of communication to establish remote correlation in the form of a desired joint distribution of actions among all nodes in a network was explored in the coordination capacity framework of \cite{cuff2010coordination .}
elements.sent-2411-015: This is especially relevant in scenarios where distributed agents must achieve decentralized cooperation (for instance in autonomous vehicle applications).
elements.sent-2411-016: Indeed, future telecommunication networks will provide services beyond the traditional role of communication of information, for which the cooperation and coordination of constituent devices will be crucial.
elements.sent-2411-017: Coordination coding can be used to model such services and obtain fundamental performance bounds that will serve as valuable benchmarks.
elements.sent-2411-018: In particular, we shall focus on the notion of strong coordination~\cite{cuff2010coordination , which requires that the distribution of the sequence of agent/node actions in the network be close in total variation to a target distribution.}
elements.sent-2411-019: Furthermore, due to the trend towards large-scale decentralized networks consisting of many mutually distrusting terminals, security and integrity of the coordinated actions are also of high priority in order to guarantee trustworthy operation.
elements.sent-2411-020: In this work, we therefore address strong coordination over a (noisy) multi-user channel by taking information-theoretic secrecy aspects into account.
elements.sent-2411-021: The fundamental limits derived and the insights drawn in this framework could be broadly applicable towards a better understanding of the information flows and efficient resource utilization in interconnected systems, such as for smart grid or telemedicine.
elements.sent-2411-022: In this paper, we address secure distributed strong coordination via a MAC-WT.
elements.sent-2411-023: In particular, there are two encoders who encode the channel inputs to the MAC-WT, and a legitimate decoder who observes one of the outputs of the MAC-WT -- see Figure~\ref{fig:encSRnoisy .}
elements.sent-2411-024: There is also an external eavesdropper who observes the other output of the MAC-WT.
elements.sent-2411-026: We will also separately analyze the case where the channel input $\tilde X_2^n$ is available non-causally to the first encoder, i.e., cribbing in the sense of~\cite[Situation 4]{willems1985discrete is allowed (Section~\ref{UPDATEDsec:SMRnoisyK0 ).}}
elements.sent-2411-027: Each encoder also has access to independent shared randomness with the legitimate decoder, which is independent of the sources and side information.
elements.sent-2411-028: Additionally, the encoders and the decoder are free to use (unconstrained) private randomization.
elements.sent-2411-029: The goal is to ensure that the decoder's output, along with the sources and side information, closely follows a target i.i.d.. p.m.f.
elements.sent-2411-030: Moreover, the secrecy requirement is such that the sources, side information and the simulated output sequence must appear to be independent of the eavesdropper channel observations.
